title: Networking Essentials
subtitle: OSI model, WebSockets, load balancers, and how data moves across networks

sections.title: The OSI Model
sections.text:
The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model is a conceptual framework that describes how data travels across a network. It has 7 layers, each with a specific job:

  1. Physical — Raw bits over wire/radio. Cables, antennas, signal encoding.
  2. Data Link — Frames between directly connected nodes. Ethernet, Wi-Fi, MAC addresses.
  3. Network — Routing between networks. IP addresses, routing protocols. This is where the Internet lives.
  4. Transport — Reliable delivery between applications. TCP, UDP, ports.
  5. Session — Manages connections. TLS handshake, authentication.
  6. Presentation — Data encoding/translation. SSL/TLS encryption, JSON parsing.
  7. Application — User-facing protocols. HTTP, DNS, FTP, SMTP.
sections.caption: The 7-layer OSI model — data moves from application to physical and back
sections.detail: The OSI model is a teaching tool, not a strict implementation guide. The Internet uses TCP/IP (a 4-layer model: Link → Internet → Transport → Application). But understanding OSI helps diagnose network issues. If you can't reach a website: layer 7 (is the server running?), layer 4 (is the port open?), layer 3 (is the IP reachable?), layer 1 (is the cable plugged in?).
sections.code:
# Diagnose network issues by OSI layer

# Layer 3 - is the host reachable?
$ ping google.com

# Layer 4 - is the port open?
$ nc -zv google.com 80

# Layer 7 - is the web server responding?
$ curl -I https://google.com

sections.title: HTTP vs WebSockets
sections.text:
HTTP is request-response: the client asks, the server answers. The connection closes after each request (in HTTP/1.1 with keep-alive, it stays open briefly, but the pattern is still request-response). This is perfect for APIs and web pages.

WebSockets are full-duplex — both client and server can send data at any time, over a single persistent connection. This is ideal for real-time applications: chat, live notifications, gaming, collaborative editing, streaming data.

The WebSocket handshake starts as HTTP (Upgrade: websocket), then switches to the WebSocket protocol. This allows WebSockets to work through standard HTTP proxies and firewalls.

items: Best for: REST APIs, web pages
Server can push data to client
Best for: chat, gaming, live data

sections.caption: HTTP for request-response, WebSockets for real-time bidirectional communication

sections.title: Load Balancers
sections.text:
Load balancers distribute incoming traffic across multiple backend servers. They're essential for scaling, high availability, and zero-downtime deployments.

Common algorithms:

  • Round Robin — Requests go to each server in turn. Simple but doesn't account for server load.
  • Least Connections — Send to the server with the fewest active connections.
  • IP Hash — Hash the client IP to determine the server. Ensures a client talks to the same server (sticky sessions).
  • Weighted — More powerful servers get more traffic.

Load balancers also perform health checks — if a server fails, they stop sending traffic to it until it recovers.
sections.caption: Load balancer distributes traffic across healthy servers, routing around failures
sections.callout: Load balancers are the first line of defense for scaling. They handle SSL termination, health checks, and can prevent the thundering herd problem during server restarts.
